source: All-In Podcast — Markets: pricing in tariffs, Trump vs Powell, the $10 trillion AGI prize
summary: The total addressable market for AI is at least $10 trillion in market cap, anchored by ~$1 trillion in annual revenue from productivity gains alone
summary_extended: Baker endorses a framework — originally proposed by Jason Calacanis — that the total addressable market for AI represents at least $10 trillion in market cap, grounded in a revenue estimate of roughly $1 trillion per year. The logic runs as follows: approximately 1 billion people in the developed world will pay around $1,000 per year for AI productivity tools, analogous to how consumers historically spent $1,000 per year on a PC (a $3,000–$4,000 machine lasting three years). Baker calls this framing 'reasonable' and 'quite reasonable,' lending it credibility as a structural sizing exercise rather than dismissing it as hype. The investment implication is that the AI sector as a whole has a long-term market cap expansion runway of at least $10 trillion, suggesting current valuations of leading AI platform companies may still be early relative to the eventual prize. Baker does not specify individual winners but implicitly frames this as a macro tailwind for the dominant AI platform and infrastructure players.
detail: Baker validates Jason Calacanis's back-of-envelope: 1 billion people in the developed world paying ~$1,000/year for AI = $1 trillion in revenue, implying ~$10 trillion in market cap. He draws analogy to the PC era where a $3-4k PC lasting 3 years = ~$1,000/year per person. He calls the framing 'reasonable' and 'quite reasonable.'
confidence_evidence: Baker explicitly validates the framework as 'reasonable' but does not independently derive it; uses hedged language around the productivity bogey
quote_fragment: the trillion dollars a year in revenue because of AI seems quite reasonable
